elements.sent-2411-001: We study the maximum set coverage problem in the massively parallel model.
elements.sent-2411-002: In this setting, $m$ sets that are subsets of a universe of $n$ elements are distributed among $m$ machines.
elements.sent-2411-003: In each round, these machines can communicate with each other, subject to the memory constraint that no machine may use more than $\tO{n}$ memory.
elements.sent-2411-004: The objective is to find the $k$ sets whose coverage is maximized.
elements.sent-2411-005: We consider the regime where $k = \Omega(m)$ (i.e., $k = m/100$), $m = O(n)$, and each machine has $\tO{n}$ memory \footnote{The input size is $O(mn)$ and each machine has the memory enough to store a constant number of sets.. .}
elements.sent-2411-006: Maximum coverage is a special case of the submodular maximization problem subject to a cardinality constraint.
elements.sent-2411-007: This problem can be approximated to within a $1-1/e$ factor using the greedy algorithm, but this approach is not directly applicable to parallel and distributed models.
elements.sent-2411-008: When $k = \Omega(m)$, to obtain a $1-1/e-\epsilon$ approximation, previous work either requires $\tO{mn}$ memory per machine which is not interesting compared to the trivial algorithm that sends the entire input to a single machine, or requires $2^{O(1/\epsilon) n$ memory per machine which is prohibitively expensive even for a moderately small value $\epsilon$.}
elements.sent-2411-009: Our result is a randomized $(1-1/e-\epsilon)$-approximation algorithm that uses \begin{equation}O(1/\epsilon^3 \cdot \log m \cdot (\log (1/\epsilon) + \log m))\end{equation} rounds.
elements.sent-2411-010: Our algorithm involves solving a slightly transformed linear program of the maximum coverage problem using the multiplicative weights update method, classic techniques in parallel computing such as parallel prefix, and various combinatorial arguments.
elements.sent-2411-011: Maximum coverage is a classic NP-Hard problem.
elements.sent-2411-012: In this problem, we have $m$ sets $S_1,S_2,\ldots,S_m$ that are subsets of a universe of $n$ elements $[n] = \{1,2,\ldots,n\}$.
elements.sent-2411-013: The goal is to find $k$ sets that cover the maximum number of elements.
elements.sent-2411-014: In the offline model, the greedy algorithm achieves a $1-1/e$ approximation and assuming $P \neq NP$, this approximation is the best possible in polynomial time \cite{Feige98 .}
elements.sent-2411-015: However, the greedy algorithm for maximum coverage and the related set cover problem is not friendly to streaming, distributed, and massively parallel computing.
elements.sent-2411-016: A large body of work has been devoted to designing algorithms for these problems in these big data computation models.

elements.algorithm-2411-001: \caption{Parallel prefix coverage} \label{alg:prefix-coverage} Compute $|S_1|, |S_2 \setminus S_1|, |S_3 \setminus (S_1 \cup S_2)|, \ldots, |S_k \setminus (S_1 \cup \ldots \cup S_{k-1})|$ in $O(\log k)$ rounds. \SetKwFunction{PrefixCoverage}{PrefixCoverage} \SetKwProg{Fn}{Function}{:}{end} \Fn{\PrefixCoverage{$S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_k$}}{ \tcp{Compute $|S_1|, |S_2 \cup S_1|, |S_3 \cup S_2 \cup S_1|, \ldots, |S_k \cup S_{k-1} \cup \ldots \cup S_1|$} \If{$k = 1$} { \Return $|S_1|$. }\Else { \tcp{Assume $k$ is even.} In one round, machine $2j - 1$ sends $S_{2j-1}$ to machine $2j$, then machine $2j$ computes $Q_{j} = S_{2j-1} \cup S_{2j}$. Run \PrefixCoverage{$Q_1, Q_2, \ldots, Q_{k/2}$} on machines $2, 4, 6, \ldots, k$. Machine $j$ now has $S_1 \cup S_2 \cup S_3 \cup \ldots \cup S_{j}$ for $j = 2, 4, 6, \ldots, k$. In one round, machine $j = 1,3,5,\ldots, k - 1$ communicates with machine $j-1$ which has $S_1 \cup S_2 \cup \ldots S_{j-1}$ and computes $S_1 \cup S_2 \cup \ldots \cup S_{j}$. \tcp{If $k$ is odd, run the above algorithm on $S_1, S_2, \ldots, S_{k-1}$ and then compute $S_1 \cup S_2 \cup \ldots \cup S_k$ in one round.} Each machine $j$ communicates with machine $j-1$ to compute $|S_1 \cup S_2 \cup \ldots \cup S_j|-|S_1 \cup S_2 \cup \ldots \cup S_{j-1}|$ in one round. } }

elements.algorithm-2411-002: \caption{Multiplicative weights for solving the LP} \label{alg:mwu} \KwIn{Objective value $L$, $\epsilon \leq 1/4$} Initialize $w_i^{(0)} = 1$ for all $i \in [n]$. \For{iteration $t=1,2,\ldots,T = O(1/\epsilon^2 \cdot \log n)$} { Run the oracle in Lemma \ref{lem:oracle} with $w^{(t-1)}$ to check if there exists a feasible solution. If the answer is Infeasible, stop the algorithm. If the answer is Feasible, let $x^{(t)}$ and $z^{(t)}$ be the output of the oracle that are now stored in all machines. \label{mwu:oracle-step}. Each machine $j$ constructs $Y_j = \{Y_{j1},Y_{j2},\ldots,Y_{jn}\}$ where $Y_{ji} = {z_j^{(t)}} \cdot [\{i \in S_j\}]$. Compute $W = \sum_{j \in [m]} Y_j$ in $O(\log m)$ rounds using the a converge-cast binary tree and send $W$ to the central machine. Note that $W_i = \sum_{S_j \ni i} {z_j^{(t)}}$. For each $i \in [n]$, the central machine computes $E_i^{(t)} = f_i \cdot error_i^{(t)}$ where \[ error_i^{(t)} := 1 - \frac{x_i^{(t)}}{f_i} - \frac{W_i}{f_i} = 1 - \frac{x_i^{(t)}}{f_i} - \sum_{S_j \ni i} \frac{z_j^{(t)}}{f_i} \ \ \ \forall i \in [n]. \] and sends $\sum_{d=1}^t E_i^{(d)}$ to all other machines. \label{MWU:step_w} For each $i \in [n]$, each machine computes $w_{i}^{(t)} = 2^{-\epsilon \cdot \sum_{d=1}^t E_i^{(d)} /f_i} = 2^{-\epsilon \cdot \sum_{d=1}^t error_i^{(d)}} = 2^{-\epsilon \cdot error_i^{(t)}} w_i^{(t-1)}$. } After $T$ iterations, output $x = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^T x^{(t)}$ and $z = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^T z^{(t)}$.

elements.sent-2411-229: Note that each batch contains $O(\log m)$ solutions.
elements.sent-2411-230: For each batch, each machine receives a message of size $\tO{k}$.
elements.sent-2411-231: For each of these $O(\log m)$ solutions in the batch, the machines can compute its coverage as follows.

elements.sent-2411-234: We do this in parallel for all solutions in the batch.
elements.sent-2411-235: Finally, we repeat this process for all $1/\epsilon$ batches.

elements.algorithm-2411-003: \caption{Multiplicative weights for solving the LP} \label{alg:mwu} \KwIn{Objective value $L$, $\epsilon \leq 1/4$} Initialize $w_i^{(0)} = 1$ for all $i \in [n]$. \For{iteration $t=1,2,\ldots,T = O(1/\epsilon^2 \cdot \log n)$} { Run the oracle in Lemma \ref{lem:oracle} with $w^{(t-1)}$ to check if there exists a feasible solution. If the answer is Infeasible, stop the algorithm. If the answer is Feasible, let $x^{(t)}$ and $z^{(t)}$ be the output of the oracle that are now stored in all machines \label{mwu:oracle-step}. Each machine $j$ constructs $Y_j = \{Y_{j1},Y_{j2},\ldots,Y_{jn}\}$ where $Y_{ji} = {z_j^{(t)}} \cdot [\{i \in S_j\}]$. Compute $W = \sum_{j \in [m]} Y_j$ in $O(\log m)$ rounds using the a converge-cast binary tree and send $W$ to the central machine. Note that $W_i = \sum_{S_j \ni i} {z_j^{(t)}}$. For each $i \in [n]$, the central machine computes $E_i^{(t)} = f_i \cdot error_i^{(t)}$ where \[ error_i^{(t)} := 1 - \frac{x_i^{(t)}}{f_i} - \frac{W_i}{f_i} = 1 - \frac{x_i^{(t)}}{f_i} - \sum_{S_j \ni i} \frac{z_j^{(t)}}{f_i} \ \ \ \forall i \in [n]. \] and sends $\sum_{d=1}^t E_i^{(d)}$ to all other machines. \label{MWU:step_w} For each $i \in [n]$, each machine computes \[ w_{i}^{(t)} = 2^{-\epsilon \cdot \sum_{d=1}^t E_i^{(d)} /f_i} = 2^{-\epsilon \cdot \sum_{d=1}^t error_i^{(d)}} = 2^{-\epsilon \cdot error_i^{(t)}} w_i^{(t-1)}. \] } After $T$ iterations, output \[ x = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^T x^{(t)} \text{, and } z = \frac{1}{T} \sum_{t=1}^T z^{(t)}. \]
